turns.content: I'm building a caching layer for my side project using the redis crate, but I keep hitting latency spikes when sending multiple writes. What if I try to batch these operations into a single pipeline instead of calling execute on each one individually?

That is exactly the right instinct because Redis pipelines reduce network round-trips which is why your latency improves so much. Think of it like writing a letter versus mailing ten separate envelopes; sending one large envelope with multiple instructions saves you from paying for ten trips to the post office. In Rust, when you call pipeline().multi(), you are essentially executing a MULTI command that marks the start of an atomic block where all subsequent commands will be treated as a single unit until EXEC is called. This atomicity ensures either everything succeeds or none do, preventing partial updates that could corrupt your cache state.

It's like sending a batch of emails at once instead of hitting send for each one individually, right? I'm trying to understand why Redis uses something called pipeline() in Rust code instead of doing requests one by one.

That analogy fits perfectly; in Rust you'd use the redis crate's Pipeline API to group commands. When you call pipeline().multi(), it creates a transaction block that sends all those operations atomically with a single round-trip, and then pipeline().exec() actually executes them together so either they all succeed or none do.
So if I make a mistake in one command inside the pipeline, does the whole batch get rolled back automatically? What happens to partial data?

Exactly; Redis guarantees atomicity for that MULTI/EXEC block. If any command fails or returns an error, you'll never see a partially applied state—either all keys are updated or nothing changes at all.
